title: Book mull and spine lining
subtitle: How to reinforce a sewn text block before casing in
excerpt: Applying book mull across the sewn spine is one of the steps that separates a durable case binding from one that will split at the hinges after a year of use. The mull bridges the gaps between signatures and gives the wing edges that glue to the inside of the cover boards.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on sewing and gluing up. The use of open-weave cloth to reinforce the spine is the standard Arts and Crafts method. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

glossaryTerms.definition: An open-weave stiffened cotton cloth (also called super) glued across the sewn spine of a text block to reinforce the joint between signatures and to provide the wing edges that attach the text block to the case boards.
A layer of paper or card glued over the mull on the spine to create a firm, flat surface before rounding or backing and before the case is attached.
The part of the book mull that extends beyond the spine edge and adheres to the inside face of the cover board at the hinge joint.

content.text: a multi-signature text block and gluing up the spine, the next stage is applying

(also called super). Mull is an open-weave cotton cloth that stiffens slightly when it absorbs PVA. It bridges the signature folds, distributes the stresses of opening across the full spine, and leaves wing edges that bond the text block to the case boards at the hinge joints.

This step happens before the book is rounded or backed, and before the case is made. If you skip it, the case will still hold initially but the hinges will fail sooner than they should, and the boards will eventually pull away from the block.

items.name: Book mull / super (slug: book-mull)
items.description: Cut a strip slightly shorter than the text block height (5 mm shorter at head and tail) and wide enough to cover the full spine plus 20-25 mm wing on each side.

items.name: PVA, acid-free (slug: pva-acid-free)
items.description: Straight from the bottle; not diluted for this step.

items.name: Bone folder (slug: bone-folder)
items.description: For pressing the mull into the spine hollows between signatures.

items.name: Scrap paper
items.description: To protect the page edges from stray PVA during gluing.

items.name: Book press or heavy boards and clamps
items.description: For holding the block flat while the PVA dries.

content.text: Place the text block spine-up between two boards in the press (or between two weights) so the spine is exposed but the boards prevent the first and last pages from bending. Brush a thin, even coat of PVA along the full length of the spine. Use the bone folder to work the PVA down into the gaps between signatures. Then apply a second coat.

Do not apply so much PVA that it seeps into the signatures. The adhesive should penetrate the top few millimetres of the folds and the thread paths, no more. The spine will feel slightly tacky but not wet to the touch when the right amount of PVA has been applied.

Lay the mull strip on a scrap paper sheet. Brush PVA across the centre of the mull (the section that will sit directly on the spine). Do not paste the wings yet. Lift the mull and centre it over the spine, pressing it down with the bone folder. The wing sections should overhang evenly on both sides.

Once the mull is positioned, use the bone folder to press it firmly into the spine, working the cloth into the small hollows between signatures. The open weave of the mull allows the PVA to key through it and bond to the spine surface underneath. Smooth the wing sections flat against the first and last pages.

Once the mull has set (about 20 minutes), cut a strip of cartridge paper (90 gsm) to the exact spine width and slightly shorter than the text block height. Brush PVA on the back of the strip and apply it over the mull, from head to tail. This spine-lining paper creates a smooth, firm surface across the spine and prevents the mull from showing through the case covering.
Place the text block in the press and press for 30 minutes. The block is now ready for rounding or backing (if either step is in the plan) and then for the case attachment.

items.symptom: Mull wings pull away from the page surface when the PVA dries
items.cause: PVA only applied to the spine section of the mull; wings not bonded
items.fix: Lift the wing and apply a fresh coat of PVA underneath; press flat and clamp.

items.symptom: PVA seeps into the pages near the spine fold
items.cause: Too much PVA applied; adhesive flowed between signatures
items.fix: When applying mull to future text blocks, use a thinner coat of PVA and apply it in two passes rather than one thick one.

items.symptom: Spine is wavy rather than flat after drying
items.cause: Text block not held in press while mull dried
items.fix: Re-dampen the spine slightly with a damp cloth, re-press, and allow to dry again under firm, even pressure.
